text:
I analyzed some concepts of gears for my math and science ebook.  I derived this helpful equation to consider: 
 The gain in speed and gain in torque are reciprocals.   For example:    gain in speed = 1 / (gain in torqure).  Im giving out a few free  copies by email.

author: @CCoburn3
text:
The Germans found out the importance of "slop" during WWII. They designed many of their weapons with truly Germanic accuracy. Then they found out that dirt gummed up the works, and the weapons were extremely hard to maintain. This made them less than ideal in the real world. (Who knew that in the real world, battlefields were dirty?) 

American engineers did the same thing with the M-16. The initial model was ALWAYS jamming because they weren't designed with dirt in mind.

So it's always a good idea to learn a lesson from engineers who came before us. In this case, the lesson we learn is to add a little "slop" into your designs so they will work (and continue to work) in the real world. Good lesson, Rob.

author: @Cooliemasteroz
text: That was a great demonstration of how to use gears. I'm no engineer but I have had practical experience due to Meccano and, in my opinion, when you are trying to increase the speed, you should have as few gears as possible as there would be greater losses due to friction compared to using it the other way around to generate high torque. If you can understand how a brake on a pushbike is more effective on the rim rather than the hub, it's the same thing. There are parallels between this and why we transmit electricity at a high voltage over long distances because a couple of volts of voltage drop is not a huge loss of power at 11,000 volts compared to how much power would be lost at 220 volts. If you lose rpm at a very high speed due to friction, then you will lose power but if used to generate torque then you probably won't lose any rpm due to friction.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
- power is where it is at, as power (energy per second) is generally where the cost is (torque x speed = power, power x time = energy  (torque without speed, or speed without torque are kind of useless (no work gets done - all big jobs need real power, being reactive doesn't help (jk) ).
.  Rob is (it appears - from his wind power discussions) really using this to demonstrate that just chucking gears at a problem doesn't always produce a solution (using equal steps helps the big kids (us and ppl like us who may not have played with Meccano) get their heads in the space practically) - showing the effort it takes just to turn those ? 8 meshes shows that converting "energy" to different forms isn't loss free - point taken, in a real system, packaging often dictates spacing and yes one uses as few conversion steps as is possible. 

cheers.

replies.author: @skampp
replies.text: You can add a ring to the back side of each gear in your design - just a couple of mm high and wide, also a spacer between the gears to reduce surface area therefore reducing friction.  I did a quick video on it in my non-channel channel but you don't need to watch, that's the gist of it.  I can tell you it's a good improvement, but I didn't measure the before/after RPM at a steady force.  I should have, sorry!  But if you haven't designed yet it's an easy addition.

author: @wanglydiaplt
text: Stumbled on your channel this morning; interesting stuff!  I've been fiddling with gears for a few months now although smaller than the ones you have here. One predicament I've come across is finding gears in the correct diametral pitch; that is to say common numbers seem to skip certain multiples. 20 is common but 40 is impossible to find for instance. I needed a 40 pitch gear to match a metric motor with a 40 pitch pinion but I couldn't find cad for anything matching. But then I realized I  could download a 20 pitch gear and scale it to 1/2 and get what I wanted. Anyway I'm sure there are other tricks so please share. :-)

author: @Boblee84
text: Nice job. Loving the gearbox vids. Great advice about the layer lines. I would also add that you can make your gear boxes even stronger by not printing your bigger and smaller gears together and bolt them together with inset nuts and bolts. The structure between the two gears is very little when printed together, essentially just the outer layer lines and the infill holding them together. When printed separately, both gears will have both top and bottom layers and the metal bolts make the connection much more rigid and able to handle the torque required to spin a load at a higher gear ratio. Also if you check out the "pro gear" option in the search bar of tinkercad you will find it has the ability to create helical cut gears which will give you even more strength and also will help it run smoother and quieter. Keep up the great vids Rob

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @shanealexander9952
text: I'm enjoying the gear series but I'm stuck in an electrical analogy.  In the electrical case, there is no gearbox.  The electrical generator has to go from 0 (or less) watts to the desired wattage in one drastic step.  Every mechanical input - output has a range limitation, especially with no gearbox.  I think preloading electrical energy through steps makes the final output more dependable.  It is supply and demand.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I m sorry mate - I don't understand that - a generator doesn't make electricity it needs mechanical input as it transforms mechanical energy into electrical energy - if a generator isn't moving it produces nothing - what It produces is in direct relation to the mechanical movement - or a least as far as I understand it

replies.author: @shanealexander9952
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Every analogy fails!  Basically saying that power should be built up in stages with different types of coils for different jobs.  The first being gathering enough resources and building some low wattage in.  The last one bumping up to the desired output.  I don't think there is enough supply which creates shortages.  And I think demand output is restricted by trying to create more supply.  
Much like the gear box,  You can't do the job with no gears, and two gears is not going to do it.  You might need several gears to get to the desired output.  (Given that you can't get out more than you put in of course)  A gear box.  
In speaker systems, just one amp can't do it all you may need a pre amp.
In water pumps, filling a tank, going from 0 psi to 200 psi, I can use 2 pumps, one for 0 to 50 and the second from 50 to 200 and increase the output by 1000% or more.  They don't make a pump that goes from 0 to 200 psi with a high output.

replies.author: @gerardguy9830
replies.text: Interesting point of view, thanks

author: @Enjoymentboy
text: I'm really glad you touched on print orientation in relation to part strength.  Most videos I see seem to ignore strength and focus primarily on print speed and using less filament.  Strength though seems to be glossed over most of the time.  Personally I'd rather a part take 50% longer to print and use more filament if it means it's less likely to break and will just last longer.  Thank you for including this info.

replies.text: Add a bit of lube and it really does make a big difference. A little surface sanding it makes it even better (very tedious).

replies.text: Also, I could see you building a mini sand blaster to quickly sand the parts!

replies.author: @Dalorath
@Dalorath
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I watched your most recent video, and was thinking. What if you experimented with determining the smallest amount of air that will travel through a tube at varying sizes of tubes? This could be translated to small tubes scattered around the intake area, feeding and filtering every element they receive. A cone at the end of each tube meshes next to other cones, would be a desired result i think, for collecting all atoms blown in...I also thought about how the tube should work, and figured that some sort of spiral shape to the cones and tubes air flow, might help with the flow of the air and molecules and atoms. The 35 Degree angle should be used but as a mesh to protect animals from being caught in the blades below. Like i said before 😀Air Weaving 😀
